title: Understand The Difference between AI, ML, and DL

content.problemStatement:
**Artificial Intelligence (AI)**
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the broad goal of making machines behave intelligently. This includes tasks such as image recognition, natural language understanding, and decision making.

AI is not a single algorithm or model. It is a field that includes many different techniques.


**Machine Learning (ML)**  
Machine Learning is one of the primary way to achieve AI. Instead of writing rules by hand, we provide data and let the model learn patterns. The model improves by seeing examples and adjusting itself based on errors.

Everything we have done so far belongs to machine learning:
- Training models using labeled data
- Making predictions
- Measuring performance using metrics such as accuracy and error rates


**Deep Learning (DL)**  
Deep Learning is a subset of machine learning. It refers to models built using neural networks with multiple layers. These layers allow the model to learn increasingly complex patterns.

The MLPs you trained are examples of deep learning models:
- Inputs are represented as numerical values
- Hidden layers learn relationships
- Outputs produce predictions

When we trained MLPs on images, time-series, and text, we were performing deep learning.




At this point, you should notice a clear structure:

**AI** is the goal.  
**ML** is how machines learn from data.  
**DL** is a powerful way to perform ML using neural networks.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
